topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The late afternoon sun slanted across my desk, illuminating dust motes dancing above my open journal. I’d left it carelessly on the corner of my nightstand, a private sanctuary filled with tangled thoughts about impending exams and a complicated crush. When I returned an hour later, the cover was slightly askew. A few pages were subtly turned, the handwriting inside familiar, yet alien. My breath hitched in my throat. I crept toward the shared room, my footsteps unnaturally loud on the hardwood floor. Leo was sprawled on his bed, ostensibly studying, but his gaze was fixed on my desk. He didn't look up when I approached, just a slow, deliberate blink. When he finally did, a faint, almost imperceptible tightening around his jaw betrayed something. "Everything alright?" he asked, his tone flat, devoid of warmth. I clutched the journal to my chest, my knuckles white. "Just... wondering why you were looking at my things."

[story 2]

It started subtly. I noticed my meticulous notes on my planner had been annotated with small, almost invisible underlines. Then came the night I found my diary open, the ink freshly drying on a page detailing a conversation I hadn't even shared with anyone. A cold knot began to form in my stomach, tightening with every passing minute. I started watching Leo more closely. Every time he entered the room, I positioned myself near the doorway, feigning interest in my laptop screen. Tonight, I saw him sitting at his desk, his head tilted, his eyes scanning the open journal. He didn't look embarrassed; he looked… satisfied. When I finally confronted him, my voice came out shaky, "Did you read my journal?" He looked up, a practiced, easy smile plastered on his face, but his eyes held a flicker of something guarded. "Read what?" he countered, leaning back in his chair, crossing his arms over his chest. The air in the room felt suddenly thin, charged with unspoken things.

[story 3]

I found myself checking the lock on my desk drawer repeatedly. It wasn't a secure lock, just a simple latch, but the need to secure my thoughts was overwhelming. The feeling of being observed had become a constant pressure, like being underwater. I caught a glimpse of Leo retreating to the bathroom, and in that brief moment of privacy, I risked a peek. The journal lay open on his bathroom counter, the pages spread out, illuminated by the harsh vanity light. My heart hammered against my ribs, a frantic drumbeat. I stayed rooted to the spot, unable to move, the silence of the apartment amplifying the frantic rhythm inside me. When he emerged, toweling his hair, he seemed entirely unruffled. "Lost something?" he asked, his voice smooth as silk. I swallowed hard, the dryness in my throat making the motion difficult. "Nothing," I managed, my gaze darting everywhere but his face. The implication hung heavy between us, a suffocating blanket.
